items.question_text: Under 10-6-1, in what airspace are the Oceanic Emergency Procedures in Section 6 to be used?
items.explanation: Per 10-6-1, the procedures in Section 6 are to be used solely in oceanic airspace.

choices.text: Solely in oceanic airspace.
In all airspace, including domestic en route and terminal.
In any airspace where an aircraft is over water, even if it is within domestic terminal boundaries.
In oceanic airspace and in Gulf of Mexico domestic airspace.

items.question_text: Where do the Oceanic Emergency Procedures of Section 6 apply?

choices.text: Solely in oceanic airspace.
In all domestic and oceanic airspace.
In any airspace where an aircraft is at least 100 NM from shore.
In oceanic airspace and also in domestic airspace for flights longer than 2 hours over water.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-1, the procedures in Section 6 are to be used solely in oceanic airspace.

items.question_text: What distinguishes the DETRESFA phase from the ALERFA phase under 10-6-2?

choices.text: DETRESFA requires reasonable certainty that the aircraft and occupants are threatened by grave and imminent danger, including fuel exhaustion or a likely forced landing.
DETRESFA is declared immediately upon any loss of communications, while ALERFA requires 60 minutes of lost contact.
ALERFA and DETRESFA have identical triggers; the distinction is made by the destination airport only.
DETRESFA applies only to cargo aircraft, while ALERFA applies to passenger flights.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-2c, a DETRESFA exists when there is reasonable certainty that the aircraft and occupants are threatened by grave and imminent danger, such as when fuel is exhausted or insufficient to reach safety, or a forced landing is likely. ALERFA (10-6-2b) is the intermediate phase with apprehension but not the grave/imminent danger required for DETRESFA.

items.question_text: Under 10-6-3, to which aircraft must alerting service be provided?

choices.text: All aircraft receiving ATC service, all other aircraft with a filed flight plan or otherwise known to the ATC unit, and any aircraft known or believed to be subject to unlawful interference.
IFR flight-plan traffic operating in oceanic airspace, excluding other known traffic.
Filed-flight-plan traffic receiving radar service at the time, excluding other ATC-service traffic.
Military traffic operating under ICAO procedures, excluding civil traffic.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-3a, alerting service is provided to all aircraft receiving ATC service, all other aircraft with a filed flight plan or otherwise known to the ATC unit, and any aircraft known or believed to be the subject of unlawful interference.

items.question_text: Under 10-6-4, what happens to the airspace after an aircraft ditching occurs?

choices.text: The RCC determines the airspace required for SAR operations, the ACC blocks that airspace until the RCC releases it, and an International NOTAM is issued describing the affected airspace.
The airspace returns to normal operations immediately after the aircraft reports ditching successfully.
Only a 5-mile radius around the ditching point is protected for 30 minutes.
The ACC independently determines the protected airspace without RCC input.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-4b, in all cases of aircraft ditching, the airspace required for SAR operations must be determined by the RCC, the ACC must block that airspace until the RCC advises it is no longer required, and an International NOTAM must be issued describing the airspace affected.

items.question_text: Under what conditions may IFR separation between a search and rescue aircraft and the aircraft in distress be discontinued?

choices.text: When visual or radar contact has been established by the SAR aircraft and the pilots of both aircraft concur.
As soon as the SAR aircraft has the distressed aircraft on TCAS.
IFR separation may never be discontinued between SAR and distressed aircraft.
Only after the distressed aircraft has landed and the SAR mission is complete.

items.explanation: Per 10-6-5a, IFR separation between the SAR aircraft and the aircraft in distress may be discontinued when visual or radar contact has been established by the search and rescue aircraft and the pilots of both aircraft concur.
